title: Implement config types and YAML parsing for collector configuration
description:
Implement the configuration layer matching Go internal/config/config.go.

Create crates/lotel-collector/src/config.rs:
- CollectorConfig struct with serde YAML deserialization:
  - receivers.otlp.protocols.grpc.endpoint (default 0.0.0.0:4317)
  - receivers.otlp.protocols.http.endpoint (default 0.0.0.0:4318)
  - processors.batch.timeout (default 1s), send_batch_size (1024), send_batch_max_size (2048)
  - exporters: file/traces, file/metrics, file/logs with path and format fields
  - extensions.health_check.endpoint (default 0.0.0.0:13133)
  - service.extensions list, service.pipelines map
- DEFAULT_CONFIG: embedded YAML string matching Go DefaultConfig exactly
  - Paths use ~/.lotel/data/ instead of /data/ (no Docker volume mapping)
- resolve_config_path() -> PathBuf:
  1. Check CWD for lotel-collector.yaml
  2. Fall back to ~/.lotel/collector-config.yaml
  3. Create default if absent
- data_path() -> PathBuf: returns ~/.lotel/data/
- Unit tests for YAML parsing of default config

Reference: internal/config/config.go (113 lines)

File reservations: crates/lotel-collector/src/config.rs

title: Initialize Rust workspace with Cargo.toml and three crate stubs
description:
Create the Rust workspace structure:
- Root Cargo.toml (workspace) with members: crates/lotel-collector, crates/lotel-storage, crates/lotel-cli
- Workspace dependencies section with shared versions:
  tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }
  tonic = "0.13"
  prost = "0.13"
  axum = "0.8"
  duckdb = { version = "1", features = ["bundled", "chrono"] }
  opentelemetry-proto = { version = "0.31", features = ["gen-tonic", "trace", "metrics", "logs", "with-serde"] }
  clap = { version = "4", features = ["derive"] }
  serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
  serde_json = "1"
  serde_yaml = "0.9"
  chrono = { version = "0.4", features = ["serde"] }
  thiserror = "2"
  anyhow = "1"
  tracing = "0.1"
  tokio-util = { version = "0.7", features = ["rt"] }
- crates/lotel-collector/Cargo.toml: tonic, prost, axum, tokio, serde, serde_json, serde_yaml, opentelemetry-proto, tracing, chrono, tokio-util, thiserror
- crates/lotel-storage/Cargo.toml: duckdb, serde, serde_json, chrono, thiserror, anyhow
- crates/lotel-cli/Cargo.toml: clap, serde_json, tokio, lotel-collector (path), lotel-storage (path), chrono, anyhow
- Each crate gets a minimal lib.rs or main.rs that compiles
- Verify: cargo build --workspace succeeds with stub crates

title: Define internal telemetry data model types shared across crates
description:
Create internal data model bridging OTLP proto types and DuckDB storage.

Create crates/lotel-collector/src/model.rs:
- SpanRecord: trace_id, span_id, parent_span_id (Option), name, kind (i32), start_time (DateTime), end_time (Option<DateTime>), duration_ns (i64), status_code (i32), service_name, attributes (serde_json::Value)
- MetricRecord: metric_name, metric_type (enum: Sum/Gauge/Histogram as string), value (f64), timestamp (DateTime), service_name, aggregation_temporality (Option<i32>), is_monotonic (Option<bool>), unit (Option<String>), attributes (serde_json::Value)
- LogRecord: timestamp (DateTime), severity (Option<String>), severity_number (Option<i32>), body (Option<String>), service_name, trace_id (Option), span_id (Option), attributes (serde_json::Value)
- Serde Serialize/Deserialize on all types for JSON output
- OtlpNano custom deserializer: handles nanosecond timestamps as either string or integer (matching Go otlpNano in internal/storage/ingest.go line 247)
- Helper: extract_service_name(resource_attributes) -> String (defaults to 'unknown')
- Helper: flatten_attrs(Vec<KeyValue>) -> serde_json::Value

title: Add unit tests for storage layer (ingest, query, prune)
description:
Add comprehensive unit tests for storage operations.

crates/lotel-storage tests (inline #[cfg(test)] modules):

Ingestion tests:
- ingest_traces with valid OTLP JSONL (fixture matching verify.py trace format)
- ingest_traces skips malformed lines without erroring
- ingest_metrics with Sum type (asInt as string '1')
- ingest_metrics with Gauge type
- ingest_logs with complete log records
- Nanosecond timestamp parsing: string and integer formats
- extract_service_name returns 'unknown' when missing

Query tests:
- query_traces returns all spans with no filters
- query_traces filters by service_name
- query_traces filters by time range (since/until)
- query_traces respects limit
- query_traces orders by start_time ASC
- query_metrics with filter combinations
- query_logs with nullable fields
- aggregate_metrics returns correct avg/min/max/count
- aggregate_metrics with no matching data returns zero count

Prune tests:
- prune dry_run reports counts without deleting
- prune deletes data older than cutoff
- prune with service filter only deletes matching service

All tests use DuckDB in temp directory via open_db().

title: Implement CLI ingest, query, and prune commands
description:
Wire storage commands into the CLI.

Update crates/lotel-cli/src/main.rs (storage handlers):

ingest command:
- Get data_path from config::data_path()
- Open DB via lotel_storage::default_db()
- Call lotel_storage::ingest_all(conn, data_path)
- Print 'Ingestion complete.'

query traces command:
- Open DB
- Parse --service, --since (parse_time), --until (parse_time), --limit into QueryOptions
- Call lotel_storage::query_traces(conn, opts)
- print_json(&results)

query metrics command:
- Same pattern, call query_metrics

query logs command:
- Same pattern, call query_logs

query aggregate command:
- Require --metric flag (eprintln error + exit 1 if missing)
- Parse QueryOptions (no limit for aggregation)
- Call lotel_storage::aggregate_metrics(conn, opts, &metric_name)
- print_json(&result)

prune command:
- Validate: exactly one of --all or --older-than required
- Parse cutoff:
  - --older-than: now - parse_duration(value)
  - --all: far future timestamp (effectively delete everything)
- Call lotel_storage::prune(conn, cutoff, service.as_deref(), dry_run)
- If dry_run: eprintln('Dry run -- no data was deleted.')
- print_json(&reports)

Reference: cmd/lotel/main.go lines 97-280

File reservations: crates/lotel-cli/src/main.rs (storage handlers)

title: Remove Docker/compose/spin/signoz runtime paths and retire Docker-specific verification scripts (outside docs)
Implement JSONL metrics ingestion into DuckDB
description:
Add metrics ingestion to crates/lotel-storage/src/ingest.rs:
- fn ingest_metrics(conn: &Connection, file: &Path) -> Result<()>
  - Read JSONL file line by line
  - Parse each line with 'resourceMetrics' array
  - For each resourceMetric -> scopeMetric -> metric:
    - Extract service.name from resource.attributes
    - Handle metric types:
      - Sum: metric_type='sum', dataPoints array, aggregationTemporality (int), isMonotonic (bool)
        - Value from dataPoints[].asDouble or asInt (asInt is a string like '1')
      - Gauge: metric_type='gauge', dataPoints array
        - Value from dataPoints[].asDouble or asInt
      - Histogram: metric_type='histogram', dataPoints array
        - Value from dataPoints[].sum
    - Parse timeUnixNano from each dataPoint
    - Extract unit from metric.unit
    - Flatten dataPoint attributes
  - INSERT INTO metrics with all fields
  - Transaction wrapping
  - Skip malformed lines

Reference: internal/storage/ingest.go lines 117-177 and 306-405

title: Analyze risks: subprocess lifecycle, stale PID state, metric temporality conversion, and file/version compatibility

comments.author: infra-admin
comments.text:
## Risk Analysis: Subprocess Lifecycle, PID State, Metric Temporality, File Compatibility

### 1. Subprocess Lifecycle Risks

**Risk: Orphaned collector process**
- Scenario: CLI crashes or is killed (SIGKILL) after spawning collector but before recording PID
- Impact: Collector runs indefinitely; ports locked; next `lotel start` fails
- Mitigation: Write PID file atomically before spawning (or immediately after fork). On start, check if PID file exists AND process is alive. If PID exists but process is dead, clean up and proceed.

**Risk: Signal forwarding**
- Scenario: User sends SIGTERM/SIGINT to lotel CLI; collector subprocess must also terminate
- Impact: If not forwarded, collector becomes orphaned
- Mitigation: Use `cmd.Process.Signal()` in Go; set up signal handler that forwards to child. Or use process groups (`Setpgid`).

**Risk: Double start**
- Scenario: User runs `lotel start` twice
- Impact: Second collector fails on port bind (4317/4318/13133 already in use)
- Mitigation: Check PID file + process liveness + port availability before starting. Return early with status message if already running.

**Risk: Unclean shutdown leaves ports bound**
- Scenario: Collector crashes without releasing ports; TIME_WAIT on TCP sockets
- Impact: Next start fails with "address already in use"
- Mitigation: Check port availability before start; if occupied, check if it's our collector (PID check) or another process.

### 2. Stale PID State Risks

**Risk: PID reuse**
- Scenario: Collector dies, PID gets reused by unrelated process, `lotel stop` kills wrong process
- Impact: Kills an innocent process
- Mitigation: Store PID + process start time (or command line) in state file. On stop, verify the process at PID is actually `otelcol` before sending signals. Example:
  ```json
  {"pid": 12345, "started_at": "2026-02-14T10:00:00Z", "binary": "otelcol-contrib"}
  ```
  Check `/proc/{pid}/cmdline` (Linux) or `ps -p {pid} -o comm=` (cross-platform).

**Risk: Stale lock/PID file after crash**
- Scenario: System crash or power loss; PID file persists but process is gone
- Impact: `lotel start` refuses to start ("already running")
- Mitigation: Always verify PID liveness, never trust file existence alone. Use `kill(pid, 0)` (signal 0) to check existence without sending a signal.

**Risk: Permission issues on PID file**
- Scenario: Collector started as different user; PID file not writable
- Impact: Cannot clean up or stop
- Mitigation: PID file should live in user-owned directory (`~/.lotel/state/`)

### 3. Metric Temporality Conversion Risks

**Risk: Delta vs Cumulative confusion**
- Context: OTLP metrics carry `aggregationTemporality` (1=DELTA, 2=CUMULATIVE)
- Scenario: Application sends cumulative counters; CLI tries to compute rate/delta
- Impact: Wrong aggregation results (e.g., avg of cumulative values is meaningless without delta conversion)
- Mitigation:
  - Store temporality metadata in DuckDB schema
  - For `avg|min|max` aggregations:
    - Gauge metrics: aggregate values directly
    - Cumulative sums: compute deltas between consecutive points before aggregating
    - Delta sums: aggregate deltas directly
    - Histograms: sum/count give average; explicit bounds give distribution
  - Emit structured warnings when mixed temporality is detected

**Risk: Histogram aggregation edge cases**
- Scenario: User queries `avg` on histogram metric
- Impact: No single "value" to average; must use sum/count
- Mitigation: For histograms, `avg` = total_sum/total_count over time window. Document this clearly. `min`/`max` are approximations from bucket boundaries.

**Risk: Empty time windows**
- Scenario: No data points in requested time range
- Impact: Division by zero in avg, null results
- Mitigation: Return explicit "no data" response with zero count, not an error

**Risk: Clock skew in timestamps**
- Scenario: Application sends metrics with timestamps significantly different from system clock
- Impact: Time-range queries miss data
- Mitigation: Use collector's received timestamp as fallback; document that queries use data point timestamps

### 4. File Format & Version Compatibility Risks

**Risk: OTLP JSON schema changes**
- Scenario: otelcol-contrib updates change JSONL output format
- Impact: Ingestion parser breaks on new fields or changed field names
- Mitigation: Use lenient JSON parsing; ignore unknown fields; test against multiple collector versions. Pin to a known collector version in recommendations.

**Risk: Parquet schema evolution**
- Scenario: Adding new columns to DuckDB schema after data already exists
- Impact: Old Parquet files lack new columns; queries fail or return null
- Mitigation: DuckDB handles missing columns as NULL automatically with `union_by_name` option. Use this.

**Risk: Concurrent read/write on JSONL files**
- Scenario: Collector appends to JSONL while ingestion reads it
- Impact: Partial line read; JSON parse error
- Mitigation: Ingestion should track file offset (or use inotify). Read only complete lines (ending with \n). For safety, use file locking or read up to last complete line.

**Risk: Large JSONL files**
- Scenario: Long-running collector with no ingestion; JSONL grows to GBs
- Impact: Ingestion takes very long; memory pressure
- Mitigation: Process JSONL incrementally (streaming). Track last-processed offset in state file. Consider rotating JSONL files periodically (collector file exporter supports rotation in newer versions).

**Risk: DuckDB version incompatibility**
- Scenario: DuckDB Go driver version changes storage format
- Impact: Cannot read old data files
- Mitigation: Use Parquet as interchange format (not DuckDB native files). Parquet is a stable, versioned format.

### Summary of Severity
| Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Priority |
|------|----------|------------|----------|
| Orphaned process | HIGH | MEDIUM | Must-fix |
| PID reuse (kill wrong process) | CRITICAL | LOW | Must-fix |
| Stale PID file | MEDIUM | HIGH | Must-fix |
| Delta/Cumulative confusion | HIGH | HIGH | Must-fix |
| Empty time window handling | LOW | MEDIUM | Should-fix |
| Concurrent JSONL read/write | MEDIUM | HIGH | Must-fix |
| Large JSONL accumulation | MEDIUM | MEDIUM | Should-fix |
| OTLP schema changes | LOW | LOW | Monitor |
| DuckDB version compat | LOW | LOW | Mitigated by Parquet |

Implement health check HTTP extension on port 13133
description:
Implement the health check extension.

Create crates/lotel-collector/src/extension/mod.rs and health.rs:
- HealthCheckExtension struct:
  - endpoint: SocketAddr (default 0.0.0.0:13133)
  - ready: Arc<AtomicBool> (shared with pipeline orchestrator)
- HTTP server (axum) serving:
  - GET / -> 200 OK when ready.load() is true
  - GET / -> 503 Service Unavailable when not ready
- HealthCheckExtension::run(self, cancel: CancellationToken) -> Result<()>
- The ready flag is set to true by the pipeline after all components are started

This endpoint is polled by:
- lotel health command (HTTP GET http://localhost:13133/)
- lotel start --wait (polls with 500ms interval, 30s timeout)
- verify.py check_collector_health()

File reservations: crates/lotel-collector/src/extension/

title: Implement CLI start, stop, status, and health commands
description:
Wire collector lifecycle commands into the CLI.

Update crates/lotel-cli/src/main.rs (collector handlers):

start command:
- Resolve config path via lotel_collector::config::resolve_config_path()
- Resolve data path via lotel_collector::config::data_path()
- Check for stale state, clean up dead PID
- Spawn self as daemon: Command::new(current_exe()).arg('run-collector').arg('--config').arg(path).arg('--data').arg(data)
  - Redirect stdout/stderr to ~/.lotel/collector.log
  - Detach (platform-specific: .spawn() + don't wait)
- Write state file ~/.lotel/collector.state: { pid, started_at, config_path, data_path }
- If --wait: poll http://localhost:13133/ every 500ms for up to 30s
- Print startup info

run-collector (hidden) command:
- Parse config from --config flag
- Create Collector::from_config_file(), call start().await
- Block until SIGTERM/SIGINT (tokio::signal)
- Call handle.shutdown()

stop command:
- Read PID from ~/.lotel/collector.state
- Send SIGTERM (nix::sys::signal::kill or std::process::Command kill)
- Wait for process exit (poll /proc/PID or waitpid with timeout)
- Remove state file
- Print 'Collector stopped.'

status command:
- Read state file, check PID alive, check health endpoint
- Output JSON: { running, healthy, uptime, pid }
- Exit code 1 if not running

health command:
- HTTP GET http://localhost:13133/ with 2s timeout
- Exit 0 if 200 OK, exit 1 otherwise

Reference: internal/collector/collector.go (295 lines)

File reservations: crates/lotel-cli/src/main.rs, crates/lotel-cli/src/daemon.rs

title: Implement collector process lifecycle for start/stop across CLI invocations
description:
Implement the background process lifecycle mechanism.

Create crates/lotel-cli/src/daemon.rs:
- pub struct CollectorState { pid: u32, started_at: String, config_path: String, data_path: String } (Serialize/Deserialize)
- State file path: ~/.lotel/collector.state

Functions:
- pub fn read_state() -> Result<Option<CollectorState>>
  - Read and parse JSON from state file, return None if absent
- pub fn write_state(state: &CollectorState) -> Result<()>
  - Serialize to JSON, write to temp file, rename atomically
- pub fn remove_state() -> Result<()>
  - Remove state file
- pub fn is_pid_alive(pid: u32) -> bool
  - Check /proc/{pid}/cmdline exists and contains 'lotel' (Linux)
  - Fallback: kill(pid, 0) == Ok (any platform with nix crate)
- pub fn stop_process(pid: u32, timeout: Duration) -> Result<()>
  - Send SIGTERM via nix::sys::signal::kill(Pid::from_raw(pid), Signal::SIGTERM)
  - Poll is_pid_alive every 100ms until dead or timeout
  - If still alive after timeout, send SIGKILL
- pub fn cleanup_stale_state() -> Result<()>
  - Read state, check PID alive, remove state if dead
- pub fn spawn_collector(config_path: &Path, data_path: &Path) -> Result<u32>
  - Build Command::new(std::env::current_exe()?)
  - Args: ["run-collector", "--config", config_path, "--data", data_path]
  - Redirect stdout/stderr to ~/.lotel/collector.log (File::create, .stdout(Stdio::from(file)))
  - .spawn() and return child.id()
  - Do NOT call .wait() (let child run independently)

title: Analyze collector config semantics, health endpoint behavior, and runtime assumptions

comments.author: infra-admin
comments.text:
## Analysis: Collector Config Semantics, Health Endpoint, Runtime Assumptions

### Config Files
Two configs exist in `docker/configs/otel/`:
1. **Full config** (`otel-collector-config.yaml`, 137 lines) — production-like
2. **Minimal config** (`otel-collector-config-minimal.yaml`, 50 lines) — file exports only

### Receivers (What to Preserve for Subprocess)
**Must keep**:
- `otlp` receiver: gRPC on `0.0.0.0:4317`, HTTP on `0.0.0.0:4318` — this is the primary ingestion path

**Drop for subprocess model**:
- `statsd` receiver (port 8125): broken anyway (no Graphite receiver), and StatsD is being deprecated
- `prometheus` scrape receiver: only scraped `localhost:8889` (self-metrics); unnecessary for file-based model

### Processors (What to Preserve)
**Must keep**:
- `batch`: 1s timeout, 1024 send size, 2048 max — standard performance optimization
- `memory_limiter`: 512 MiB limit, 128 MiB spike — important for subprocess resource control

**Drop or simplify**:
- `attributes` processor: hardcodes `service.name: canary-api` — this was demo-specific; for real usage, applications set their own service.name
- `filter` processor: includes only `canary\.*`, `otel\.*`, `system\.*` metrics — too restrictive for general use
- `resource` processor: hardcodes `host.name: local-dev` — demo-specific

### Exporters (What to Preserve)
**Must keep**:
- `file/traces`: path `/data/traces/traces.jsonl`, format json
- `file/metrics`: path `/data/metrics/metrics.jsonl`, format json
- `file/logs`: path `/data/logs/logs.jsonl`, format json

**Drop**:
- `file/traces_json`: duplicate traces output (`traces_detailed.json`)
- `prometheus` exporter: no Prometheus service in subprocess model
- `otlp/jaeger` exporter: no Jaeger service in subprocess model
- `debug` exporter: optional, can be added as verbose flag

### Extensions
**Must keep**:
- `health_check` on `0.0.0.0:13133` — critical for readiness checks

**Drop**:
- `pprof` on `0.0.0.0:1777` — not needed for local dev
- `zpages` on `0.0.0.0:55679` — not needed for local dev

### Health Endpoint Behavior
- Endpoint: `http://localhost:13133/`
- Returns HTTP 200 when collector is ready (all pipelines initialized)
- Docker health check: `curl -f http://localhost:13133/` every 30s, 10s timeout, 3 retries
- For subprocess model: poll this endpoint after spawning to confirm readiness
- The health endpoint is provided by the `health_check` extension, which is standard in otelcol-contrib

### Service Pipelines (Preserve These Semantics)
```yaml
traces:   otlp → [memory_limiter, batch] → file/traces
metrics:  otlp → [memory_limiter, batch] → file/metrics
logs:     otlp → [memory_limiter, batch] → file/logs
```
Stripped of demo-specific processors (attributes, filter, resource).

### Runtime Assumptions
1. **Port availability**: 4317, 4318, 13133 must be free on localhost
2. **File system**: collector writes JSONL to data directories; files grow unbounded without pruning
3. **JSONL format**: each line is a complete OTLP JSON object (resourceSpans/resourceMetrics/resourceLogs)
4. **No rotation**: collector's file exporter appends indefinitely; no built-in rotation
5. **Crash recovery**: collector restarts cleanly; no WAL or transaction log — data in batch buffer is lost on crash (acceptable for local dev)
6. **Binary requirement**: subprocess model needs `otelcol-contrib` binary installed on PATH or at a known location

### Recommended Minimal Subprocess Config
```yaml
receivers:
  otlp:
    protocols:
      grpc: { endpoint: "0.0.0.0:4317" }
      http: { endpoint: "0.0.0.0:4318" }
processors:
  batch:
    timeout: 1s
    send_batch_size: 1024
    send_batch_max_size: 2048
exporters:
  file/traces:
    path: ${DATA_DIR}/traces/traces.jsonl
    format: json
  file/metrics:
    path: ${DATA_DIR}/metrics/metrics.jsonl
    format: json
  file/logs:
    path: ${DATA_DIR}/logs/logs.jsonl
    format: json
extensions:
  health_check:
    endpoint: 0.0.0.0:13133
service:
  extensions: [health_check]
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [batch]
      exporters: [file/traces]
    metrics:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [batch]
      exporters: [file/metrics]
    logs:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [batch]
      exporters: [file/logs]
```

title: Implement OTLP HTTP receiver on port 4318
description:
Implement the OTLP HTTP receiver using axum.

Create crates/lotel-collector/src/receiver/http.rs:
- POST /v1/traces: accept JSON body (ExportTraceServiceRequest), forward to channel
- POST /v1/metrics: accept JSON body (ExportMetricsServiceRequest), forward to channel
- POST /v1/logs: accept JSON body (ExportLogsServiceRequest), forward to channel
- Content-Type: application/json (required for verify.py compatibility)
- Optional: application/x-protobuf support via prost::Message::decode
- Return HTTP 200 with empty JSON body on success
- Return 400 for malformed requests, 500 for internal errors
- OtlpHttpReceiver struct with:
  - new(endpoint: SocketAddr, tx: mpsc::Sender<SignalData>) -> Self
  - serve(self, cancel: CancellationToken) -> Result<()>
- Share the same mpsc::Sender as gRPC receiver (unified data path)
- Graceful shutdown via axum::serve with_graceful_shutdown

The HTTP endpoint is what verify.py uses:
  requests.post(f'{OTLP_HTTP}/v1/traces', json=data)
  requests.post(f'{OTLP_HTTP}/v1/metrics', json=data)
  requests.post(f'{OTLP_HTTP}/v1/logs', json=data)

title: Implement batch processor with configurable timeout and size limits
description:
Implement the batch processor matching OTel Collector batch semantics.

Create crates/lotel-collector/src/processor/mod.rs and batch.rs:
- BatchProcessor struct:
  - config: timeout (Duration), send_batch_size (usize), send_batch_max_size (usize)
  - Defaults: timeout=1s, send_batch_size=1024, send_batch_max_size=2048
- Receives SignalData from receiver channel (tokio::mpsc::Receiver)
- Accumulates items in internal buffer
- Flushes to exporter channel (tokio::mpsc::Sender) when:
  1. Buffer reaches send_batch_size items, OR
  2. Timeout expires since last flush (tokio::time::interval)
  3. Buffer exceeds send_batch_max_size -> immediate flush
- BatchProcessor::run(self, rx: Receiver, tx: Sender, cancel: CancellationToken) -> Result<()>
- Graceful shutdown: on cancel signal, flush remaining buffered items
- Handle all three signal types (Traces, Metrics, Logs) through the same processor
- Unit tests:
  - Send exactly send_batch_size items, verify flush
  - Send fewer items, wait for timeout, verify flush
  - Verify shutdown flushes remaining items

File reservations: crates/lotel-collector/src/processor/

    - If not dry_run and count > 0: DELETE FROM {table} WHERE {time_col} < ? [AND service_name = ?]
    - Build PruneReport with signal name, service, count, cutoff as RFC3339
  - Return Vec of 3 PruneReport entries

  - Prune for real, verify deletion
  - Prune with service filter

title: Implement metrics query command with service.name filtering and absolute/relative time windows
Analyze current lotel CLI command structure and Docker-coupled start/stop behavior

comments.author: infra-admin
comments.text:
## Analysis: Current CLI Command Structure & Docker-Coupled Start/Stop

### CLI Entrypoint
- **File**: `cmd/lotel/main.go` (61 lines)
- **Framework**: Cobra CLI + Charmbracelet Fang for execution
- **Commands**: Only `start` and `stop` exist today

### Command Flow

**`lotel start`**:
1. Calls `config.ResolvePaths()` to get config file path + data directory
2. Creates Docker API client via `docker.NewClient()`
3. Calls `dc.StartCollector(ctx, configPath, dataPath)`
4. StartCollector: pulls image → checks for existing container → ensures network → creates container with port bindings and volume mounts → starts container

**`lotel stop`**:
1. Creates Docker API client
2. Calls `dc.StopCollector(ctx)`
3. StopCollector: lists all containers → finds by name → stops (10s timeout) → removes container

### Docker Coupling Points
- `internal/docker/docker.go` (173 lines): All orchestration lives here
  - Uses `github.com/docker/docker` client SDK directly
  - Creates `telemetry-nest-network` bridge network
  - Manages container named `telemetry-nest-otel-collector`
  - Pulls `otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib:latest`
  - Binds ports 4317, 4318, 13133, 8889
  - Mounts config at `/etc/otel-collector-config.yaml`
  - Mounts data at `/data` from `~/.lotel/data`
  - Sets health check: `curl -f http://localhost:13133/`
  - Restart policy: `unless-stopped`

- `internal/config/config.go` (41 lines): Path resolution and constants
  - `ImageName = "otel/opentelemetry-collector-contrib:latest"`
  - `ContainerName = "telemetry-nest-otel-collector"`
  - `NetworkName = "telemetry-nest-network"`
  - Config path: `{cwd}/docker/configs/otel/otel-collector-config.yaml`
  - Data path: `~/.lotel/data/{traces,metrics,logs}`

### Dependencies (go.mod)
- `github.com/docker/docker v28.5.2` — Docker API client
- `github.com/docker/go-connections v0.6.0` — NAT port bindings
- These are the ONLY Docker-specific deps; removing them is straightforward

### Migration Path to Subprocess Model
1. Replace `internal/docker/` with `internal/process/` or `internal/collector/`
2. Use `os/exec` to spawn `otelcol-contrib` binary as subprocess
3. Store PID in `~/.lotel/state/collector.pid` for lifecycle management
4. Config file can remain at same path or move to `~/.lotel/config/`
5. Remove Docker deps from go.mod
6. Add `status` command (not present today)
7. Health check should use same HTTP endpoint: `http://localhost:13133/`

### No Other Commands Exist
- No `query`, `prune`, `status`, `health` commands today
- The CLI is a thin wrapper around Docker container lifecycle
- No state management beyond container existence

title: Add verification for direct OTLP ingestion (trace+metric+log) using UUID service.name and query assertions
Analyze Docker-era verification/test assets and define replacement scope for Python end-to-end flow

comments.author: infra-admin
comments.text:
## Analysis: Docker-Era Verification/Test Assets & Replacement Scope

### Current Verification Assets

**1. Bash health check** (`scripts/verification/bash/check_telemetry_health.sh`, 241 lines)
- Checks 6 Docker containers running (otel-collector, statsd, prometheus, grafana, jaeger, filebeat)
- Checks service endpoints: OTel health, Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger, StatsD admin
- Checks data directories exist and are writable
- Checks container-to-container network connectivity (docker exec nc)
- Checks config files exist
- Checks for recent telemetry data files
- Queries Prometheus for active targets
- **Docker dependencies**: docker ps, docker exec, docker-compose
- **Verdict**: REPLACE entirely — subprocess model has no containers to check

**2. Python pipeline test** (`scripts/verification/python/test_metrics_pipeline.py`, 409 lines)
- `check_services_health()`: HTTP checks on OTel, Prometheus, Grafana, Jaeger
- `send_statsd_metrics()`: UDP to localhost:8125 (broken)
- `send_otlp_traces()`: HTTP POST to localhost:4318/v1/traces
- `send_otlp_metrics()`: HTTP POST to localhost:4318/v1/metrics
- `verify_file_exports()`: checks data/ files exist and have content
- `check_prometheus_metrics()`: queries Prometheus API
- **Docker dependencies**: assumes Docker stack is running, checks Prometheus/Grafana/Jaeger
- **Reusable parts**: OTLP trace/metric submission code structure
- **Verdict**: REPLACE — keep OTLP submission patterns but rewrite verification against CLI queries

**3. Go trace generator** (`scripts/verification/go/trace_generator.go`, 293 lines)
- Generates realistic Canary API traces with random IDs, parent-child relationships
- Sends via HTTP POST to localhost:4318/v1/traces
- **Docker dependencies**: none (pure OTLP HTTP)
- **Verdict**: KEEP as optional smoke-test tool; not critical path

**4. Shell setup/start/stop scripts** (`scripts/setup/`, ~630 lines total)
- `setup-telemetry-env.sh`: Docker prereqs, network, volumes, image pulls
- `start-telemetry-stack.sh`: docker-compose up with dependency ordering
- `stop-telemetry-stack.sh`: docker-compose down with cleanup
- **Verdict**: REMOVE — replaced by `lotel start/stop`

**5. Example apps** (`examples/python-fastapi/`, `examples/distributed/`)
- Self-contained Docker Compose apps that send telemetry
- Not verification per se, but reference implementations
- **Verdict**: KEEP in docs/examples — they work against any OTLP endpoint

### Replacement: Python End-to-End Verification Flow

**New script**: `scripts/verify.py` (or `tests/e2e/test_verify.py`)

**Flow**:
1. **Check collector**: `lotel status` returns healthy (or `curl http://localhost:13133/`)
2. **Generate UUID service tag**: `test-verify-{uuid4()}`
3. **Send OTLP traces**: POST to localhost:4318/v1/traces with UUID service.name
4. **Send OTLP metrics**: POST to localhost:4318/v1/metrics with UUID service.name
5. **Send OTLP logs**: POST to localhost:4318/v1/logs with UUID service.name
6. **Wait for ingestion**: sleep or poll
7. **Query traces**: `lotel query traces --service {uuid}` → assert non-empty JSON
8. **Query metrics**: `lotel query metrics --service {uuid}` → assert non-empty JSON
9. **Query logs**: `lotel query logs --service {uuid}` → assert non-empty JSON
10. **Test prune**: `lotel prune --older-than 0h --service {uuid} --dry-run` → assert lists files
11. **Execute prune**: `lotel prune --older-than 0h --service {uuid}` → assert deletion
12. **Verify deletion**: `lotel query traces --service {uuid}` → assert empty

**Dependencies**: Only `requests` (for OTLP HTTP) + `subprocess` (for CLI commands)
**No Docker dependency at all.**

### Assets to Remove
- `scripts/verification/bash/check_telemetry_health.sh`
- `scripts/verification/python/test_metrics_pipeline.py`
- `scripts/setup/setup-telemetry-env.sh`
- `scripts/setup/start-telemetry-stack.sh`
- `scripts/setup/stop-telemetry-stack.sh`

### Assets to Keep
- `scripts/verification/go/trace_generator.go` — optional, no Docker dep
- `examples/` — reference apps, not verification
- `docs/` — reference material

title: Finalize DuckDB embedding plan (driver/build constraints, schema boundaries, and migration notes)

comments.author: infra-admin
comments.text:
## DuckDB Embedding Plan

### Driver Selection
- **Package**: github.com/duckdb/duckdb-go/v2 (official, maintained by DuckDB team)
- **Interface**: standard database/sql — no vendor lock-in
- **Build**: uses CGO (C library embedded); cross-compiles via standard Go toolchain
- **Version**: latest duckdb-go v2.5.5+ (DuckDB v1.4.4)

### Build Constraints
- CGO_ENABLED=1 required (default on most systems)
- Linux amd64/arm64: works out of the box
- macOS amd64/arm64: works out of the box
- Windows: not a target (per constraints)
- Binary size increase: ~30-50MB due to embedded DuckDB C library

### Architecture Decision: In-Process DuckDB (not Parquet files)
After analysis, the simpler approach is better:
- Use a single DuckDB database file at ~/.lotel/data/lotel.db
- DuckDB supports concurrent reads from multiple processes
- Writes are serialized via DuckDB's internal WAL
- No need for a separate Parquet ingestion pipeline
- DuckDB can directly query JSONL via read_json() for initial import

### Schema Boundaries
Three tables, one per signal:

**traces**: spans extracted from OTLP resourceSpans JSONL
**metrics**: data points extracted from OTLP resourceMetrics JSONL
**logs**: log records extracted from OTLP resourceLogs JSONL

Each table partitioned by (service_name, date) using DuckDB partition columns.
This gives efficient pruning: DELETE FROM traces WHERE service_name = ? AND date < ?

### Migration Notes
- Phase 1: JSONL ingestion into DuckDB on query (lazy load)
- Phase 2: Background ingestion after start (eager load)
- No schema migration framework needed initially — CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS
- If schema changes, drop and re-ingest from JSONL source files

### Why Not Parquet Files
- Adds ingestion pipeline complexity
- DuckDB can query JSONL directly for small-to-medium datasets
- For local dev telemetry volumes, DuckDB in-process is more than fast enough
- Simpler pruning via SQL DELETE vs directory tree management

title: Implement traces query command with service.name first-class filtering and absolute/relative time windows
Implement collector subprocess lifecycle in CLI (start/stop/status) with crash-safe PID/state handling
Implement signal/service/date partition manager at $HOME/.lotel/data/<signal>/service.name=<svc>/YYYY/MM/DD
Analyze current telemetry disk outputs and define DuckDB-backed signal/service/date partition contract under $HOME/.lotel/data

comments.author: infra-admin
comments.text:
## Analysis: Telemetry Disk Outputs & DuckDB Partition Contract

### Current Disk Output Format

**Location**: `~/.lotel/data/{traces,metrics,logs}/`

**File format**: JSONL (JSON Lines) — one JSON object per line
- `traces.jsonl`: Each line is an OTLP `resourceSpans` export batch
- `metrics.jsonl`: Each line is an OTLP `resourceMetrics` export batch
- `logs.jsonl`: Each line is an OTLP `resourceLogs` export batch

**Sample OTLP trace line structure**:
```json
{"resourceSpans":[{"resource":{"attributes":[{"key":"service.name","value":{"stringValue":"my-svc"}}]},"scopeSpans":[{"spans":[{"traceId":"...","spanId":"...","name":"GET /foo","startTimeUnixNano":"...","endTimeUnixNano":"...","attributes":[...]}]}]}]}
```

**Current issues with flat JSONL**:
1. Single file per signal — grows unbounded
2. No partitioning by service or date — full scan for any query
3. No indexing — O(n) for any lookup
4. Mixed services in same file — can't prune per-service
5. Append-only from collector — no rotation

### Proposed DuckDB Partition Contract

**Root**: `$HOME/.lotel/data/`

**Physical layout** (Hive-style partitioning):
```
$HOME/.lotel/data/
├── traces/
│   └── service.name=my-svc/
│       └── 2026/02/14/
│           └── data.parquet    (or DuckDB page file)
├── metrics/
│   └── service.name=my-svc/
│       └── 2026/02/14/
│           └── data.parquet
└── logs/
    └── service.name=my-svc/
        └── 2026/02/14/
            └── data.parquet
```

**Why this layout**:
- Service-first: enables per-service pruning and queries (most common filter)
- Date partitioned: enables time-range queries and age-based pruning
- Compatible with DuckDB's Hive partitioning reads
- Human-navigable for debugging

### DuckDB Approach Options

**Option A: DuckDB as embedded query engine over Parquet files**
- Ingestion: parse JSONL → write Parquet files partitioned by service+date
- Query: DuckDB reads Parquet files using Hive partitioning
- Pros: Files are portable, no DB lock contention, can prune by deleting directories
- Cons: Need a separate ingestion step; Parquet write adds complexity

**Option B: DuckDB as single database file**
- Single `~/.lotel/data/lotel.duckdb` file
- Pros: Simpler writes, atomic transactions, built-in indexing
- Cons: File locking (only one process can write), harder to prune, less transparent

**Recommended: Option A (Parquet + DuckDB query engine)**
Reasons:
- Collector subprocess writes JSONL (no change needed to collector)
- A background ingestion routine or CLI command parses JSONL → Parquet
- DuckDB queries Parquet files at read time with partition pruning
- Pruning = `rm -rf` on date directories (simple, safe, atomic per-directory)
- No lock contention between collector writes and CLI queries

### Ingestion Pipeline
```
Collector → ~/.lotel/data/raw/{traces,metrics,logs}.jsonl  (append-only JSONL)
                    ↓ (lotel ingest, or triggered by query)
            ~/.lotel/data/{signal}/service.name={svc}/YYYY/MM/DD/data.parquet
                    ↓ (lotel query)
            DuckDB reads Parquet with Hive partition pruning
```

### Schema Contracts

**Traces table** (per partition file):
| Column | Type | Source |
|--------|------|--------|
| trace_id | VARCHAR | span.traceId |
| span_id | VARCHAR | span.spanId |
| parent_span_id | VARCHAR | span.parentSpanId |
| name | VARCHAR | span.name |
| kind | INTEGER | span.kind |
| start_time | TIMESTAMP | span.startTimeUnixNano |
| end_time | TIMESTAMP | span.endTimeUnixNano |
| duration_ns | BIGINT | computed |
| status_code | INTEGER | span.status.code |
| service_name | VARCHAR | resource.attributes[service.name] |
| attributes | JSON | span.attributes (flattened) |

**Metrics table**:
| Column | Type | Source |
|--------|------|--------|
| metric_name | VARCHAR | metric.name |
| metric_type | VARCHAR | sum/gauge/histogram/summary |
| value | DOUBLE | dataPoint value |
| timestamp | TIMESTAMP | dataPoint.timeUnixNano |
| service_name | VARCHAR | resource.attributes[service.name] |
| aggregation_temporality | INTEGER | 1=delta, 2=cumulative |
| is_monotonic | BOOLEAN | for sums |
| attributes | JSON | dataPoint.attributes |

**Logs table**:
| Column | Type | Source |
|--------|------|--------|
| timestamp | TIMESTAMP | logRecord.timeUnixNano |
| severity | VARCHAR | logRecord.severityText |
| severity_number | INTEGER | logRecord.severityNumber |
| body | VARCHAR | logRecord.body.stringValue |
| service_name | VARCHAR | resource.attributes[service.name] |
| trace_id | VARCHAR | logRecord.traceId |
| span_id | VARCHAR | logRecord.spanId |
| attributes | JSON | logRecord.attributes |

### Pruning Contract
- `lotel prune --older-than 7d` → deletes all partition directories older than 7 days
- `lotel prune --older-than 24h --service my-svc` → scoped to one service
- Dry-run mode: list directories/files/bytes that would be deleted
- No DuckDB involvement for pruning — pure filesystem operations on partition directories

    - Extract service.name from resource.attributes

title: Implement JSONL file exporter for traces, metrics, and logs
description:
Implement the file exporter that writes OTLP data as JSONL.

Create crates/lotel-collector/src/exporter/mod.rs and file.rs:
- FileExporter struct:
  - traces_path: PathBuf (default ~/.lotel/data/traces/traces.jsonl)
  - metrics_path: PathBuf (default ~/.lotel/data/metrics/metrics.jsonl)
  - logs_path: PathBuf (default ~/.lotel/data/logs/logs.jsonl)
- FileExporter::run(self, rx: Receiver<SignalData>, cancel: CancellationToken) -> Result<()>
- On receiving SignalData:
  - Match on variant (Traces/Metrics/Logs)
  - Serialize the proto request to JSON using serde (with-serde feature)
  - Append as single line to appropriate JSONL file (open in append mode)
  - Each line must be valid JSON ending with newline
- Output format must match OTel Collector file exporter:
  - Traces: {"resourceSpans":[...]}
  - Metrics: {"resourceMetrics":[...]}
  - Logs: {"resourceLogs":[...]}
- Create parent directories if absent (std::fs::create_dir_all)
- Use BufWriter for performance
- Flush on shutdown

title: Implement DuckDB connection management and schema migration
description:
Implement the DuckDB storage foundation in lotel-storage.

Create crates/lotel-storage/src/db.rs:
- pub fn open_db(path: &Path) -> Result<Connection>
  - Open DuckDB at given path, run migration
  - Create parent directories if absent
- pub fn default_db() -> Result<Connection>
  - Opens at ~/.lotel/data/lotel.db
- fn migrate(conn: &Connection) -> Result<()>
  - CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS traces (
      trace_id VARCHAR NOT NULL, span_id VARCHAR NOT NULL, parent_span_id VARCHAR,
      name VARCHAR NOT NULL, kind INTEGER, start_time TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
      end_time TIMESTAMP, duration_ns BIGINT, status_code INTEGER,
      service_name VARCHAR NOT NULL, attributes JSON, date DATE NOT NULL)
  - CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS metrics (
      metric_name VARCHAR NOT NULL, metric_type VARCHAR NOT NULL, value DOUBLE,
      timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, service_name VARCHAR NOT NULL,
      aggregation_temporality INTEGER, is_monotonic BOOLEAN, unit VARCHAR,
      attributes JSON, date DATE NOT NULL)
  - CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS logs (
      timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, severity VARCHAR, severity_number INTEGER,
      body VARCHAR, service_name VARCHAR NOT NULL, trace_id VARCHAR,
      span_id VARCHAR, attributes JSON, date DATE NOT NULL)
- Unit tests: open in-memory DB, run migration, verify tables exist via information_schema

Reference: internal/storage/db.go (108 lines) - exact schema match

title: Implement pipeline orchestration connecting receivers -> processor -> exporters
description:
Wire all collector components into a running pipeline.

Create crates/lotel-collector/src/pipeline.rs:
- Pipeline struct owning all components and channels
- Pipeline::new(config: &CollectorConfig) -> Result<Self>:
  - Create tokio::mpsc channels (receiver->processor, processor->exporter)
  - Instantiate OtlpGrpcReceiver (bound to config gRPC endpoint)
  - Instantiate OtlpHttpReceiver (bound to config HTTP endpoint)
  - Instantiate BatchProcessor (with config batch params)
  - Instantiate FileExporter (with config paths)
  - Instantiate HealthCheckExtension (with config endpoint, shared Arc<AtomicBool>)
  - Create shared CancellationToken for coordinated shutdown
- Pipeline::run(self) -> Result<PipelineHandle>:
  - Spawn each component as a tokio task via tokio::spawn
  - Set health check readiness to true after all tasks spawned
  - Return PipelineHandle with CancellationToken and JoinHandle vec
- PipelineHandle::shutdown(self) -> Result<()>:
  - Cancel the CancellationToken
  - Await all JoinHandles for graceful drain
- Error propagation: if any component task panics/errors, cancel all others

title: Add telemetrygen ingestion smoke validation for traces/metrics/logs as blocking verification
Implement JSONL traces ingestion into DuckDB
description:
Implement traces ingestion from OTLP JSONL files into DuckDB.

Create crates/lotel-storage/src/ingest.rs (traces portion):
- pub fn ingest_all(conn: &Connection, data_path: &Path) -> Result<()>
  - Calls ingest_traces, ingest_metrics, ingest_logs for respective subdirs
  - Skips missing files/directories gracefully
- fn ingest_traces(conn: &Connection, file: &Path) -> Result<()>
  - Read JSONL file line by line (BufReader with large buffer)
  - Parse each line as serde_json::Value with 'resourceSpans' array
  - For each resourceSpan -> scopeSpan -> span:
    - Extract service.name from resource.attributes (key='service.name', value.stringValue)
    - Parse startTimeUnixNano and endTimeUnixNano (handle both string and integer formats)
    - Compute duration_ns = end - start
    - Flatten span attributes to JSON
    - Extract: traceId, spanId, parentSpanId, name, kind, status.code
  - INSERT INTO traces (trace_id, span_id, ..., date) VALUES (?, ?, ..., ?)
  - Use transaction (BEGIN/COMMIT) for batch performance
  - Skip malformed lines (log warning, continue)
- OTLP nanosecond timestamp: deserialize as either string or integer (u64)
  - Convert to chrono::NaiveDateTime
  - Extract date component for partition key

Reference: internal/storage/ingest.go lines 38-115 (ingestTraces)
